blurb: A bulletproof native fruiting shrub — clouds of white spring stars give way to sweet, blueberry-like blue-purple pomes that bake into the best pies on the High Plains, on a tough thicket-former that shrugs off cold, drought, and alkaline clay.
sun: Full sun to part shade; fruits best in full sun
water: Low to moderate once established; drought-tolerant but sweetest fruit with occasional deep summer water
spread: Slowly thicket-forming by rhizomes and root suckers — colonizes over time but easily kept in bounds
seasons: White flower clusters Apr–May; blue-purple edible pomes ripen Jun–Jul; orange-red fall foliage; bare grey multi-stem framework in winter
wildlife: Spring flowers feed native bees and butterflies (larval host for elfins and orange-tips); fruit is a songbird and small-mammal favorite
deer: Readily browsed by deer and other big game, especially the twigs in winter — protect young plants in deer country
toxic: Ripe fruit is edible and prized; like all rosaceous stone/pome fruit the seeds, leaves, and twigs contain cyanogenic glycosides and are toxic in quantity to people and livestock

care.hardiness: Extremely cold hardy (USDA zone 2–3) and native across Colorado's mountains and foothills up to roughly 9,000 ft, so the Front Range is well within its comfort zone — no winter protection needed. It's drought-resistant and tolerates the region's alkaline soils, which is exactly why CSU lists it as ideal for the Rocky Mountain and High Plains.[1][3]
care.planting: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped stock in early spring (Apr–May) or early fall (Sep) so roots establish before heat or hard frost. 'Smoky' is the classic Canadian fruiting selection; plant at least two plants nearby, as fruit set improves with cross-pollination even though serviceberry is largely self-fruitful. In containers it's far less cold-hardy at the roots and dries out fast — if grown in a pot, use the largest possible, water often, and sink the pot or move it to an unheated shelter for winter; it's much happier in the ground.[2][3]
care.sun: Grows in full sun, part shade, or even fairly deep shade, but flowers and fruits most heavily in full sun (6+ hrs). On hot, dry Front Range sites a bit of afternoon shade reduces water stress without sacrificing much fruit.[1][3]
care.soil: Adaptable to most well-drained soils, including the region's alkaline clay; tolerates infertile ground but grows best on mesic, well-drained loam amended with compost. It does not tolerate prolonged waterlogging or extended drought — aim for evenly moist but never soggy.[2][3]
care.water: Drought-resistant once established, but the sweetest, largest fruit comes with consistent moisture through bloom and fruit-set; water deeply every 7–14 days in summer heat the first few years, then mainly during dry spells. Mulch to conserve soil moisture.[2][3]
care.spacing: Space plants 6–8 ft apart for a fruiting hedge or thicket; mature plants reach 6–15 ft tall depending on cultivar and moisture. Allow room for slow rhizomatous spread, or site where stray suckers can be mowed off.[1][3]
care.propagation: By seed, cold-moist stratify the cleaned seed 90–120 days at 33–41°F before sowing; germination is slow and variable, so named selections like 'Smoky' are kept true vegetatively. Easiest non-seed methods are digging and transplanting rooted suckers in early spring, layering, softwood cuttings in summer, or hardwood cuttings in early spring.[1][2]
care.bloom: Showy clusters of white, five-petaled flowers with characteristically strappy, well-separated petals open April–May, often before the leaves fully expand. The display is brief but striking and is the plant's main pollinator event.[1][3]
care.feeding: Generally undemanding; a light topdressing of compost or balanced slow-release fertilizer in early spring supports fruiting on lean soils. Avoid heavy nitrogen, which pushes leafy growth at the expense of fruit.[3]
care.maintenance: Low maintenance. Prune in late winter while dormant to remove dead, weak, or crossing stems and to open the canopy for light and air; renew old thickets by cutting the oldest stems to the ground over a few years. Remove unwanted suckers to control spread.[1][2]
care.troubles: Like other pome fruits it can get rust (where junipers are nearby), powdery mildew, leaf spot, fire blight, and occasional borers; most are cosmetic in a home planting. Birds are the biggest competitor for ripe fruit — net plants you want to harvest.[1][2]
care.harvest: Berries ripen Jun–Jul, turning from red to deep blue-purple/black and softening when fully sweet. Pick by hand or strip into a bucket; eat fresh or use in pies, muffins, jams, syrups, or dried like raisins. Birds strip ripe fruit fast, so harvest promptly or net.[1][3]
care.companions: Pairs well with other tough native shrubs (chokecherry, Wood's rose, golden currant) in a wildlife hedge, and with prairie perennials and grasses at its feet; useful as the structural backbone of a low-water edible/pollinator border.[3]

edible.summary: The ripe blue-purple pomes are a choice wild edible — sweet, blueberry-like with a hint of almond — eaten fresh or baked; the seeds, leaves, and twigs are not food.[1][3]
edible.parts: The edible part is the ripe pome — the soft, sweet, blue-purple to nearly black fruit that ripens June–July. It's often compared to blueberry but with a slight apple flavor, and is high in iron, potassium, and manganese. Indigenous peoples ate the fruit fresh, dried it like raisins, and pounded it into pemmican; it's classic pie, jelly, syrup, and muffin fruit. The leaves, twigs, bark, and seeds are not eaten as food.[1][2][3]
edible.preparation: Eat the ripe fruit fresh off the bush, or cook it into pies, muffins, jams, syrups, and sauces; it also dries well for winter storage. Ordinary use of the soft ripe flesh is safe, but don't deliberately crush, grind, or eat quantities of the hard seeds, and don't brew 'tea' from the leaves or twigs.[1][3]
edible.caution: Like all rosaceous stone- and pome-fruit (Prunus, Amelanchier, etc.), the seeds, leaves, and especially young twigs contain cyanogenic glycosides that release hydrogen cyanide (HCN); the glycosides are most concentrated in young twigs and least in older leaves. The ripe FLESH is the edible part. Do not brew the leaves/twigs or eat quantities of crushed seed, and never let livestock graze it as sole forage — a diet consisting solely of serviceberry can be fatal to ruminants, and CSU notes the plant is toxic to livestock. Wilted foliage is the highest-risk material. Keep children from chewing twigs or leaves.[2][3]
